chunk_summary: Discharge follow-up for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) must include structured micronutrient plans, community integration, and reassessment to prevent relapse and ensure recovery.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of structured micronutrient supplementation plans post-discharge in SAM care.
Identify key follow-up assessments, including anthropometry, morbidity, and micronutrient adherence.
Learn how to integrate SAM recovery plans with community health programs and prophylaxis schedules.
Recognize when to evaluate for underlying conditions in cases of non-response to rehabilitation.
key_facts: Micronutrient care must continue post-discharge to prevent relapse and missed supplementation.
Iron supplementation should only be restarted during the rehabilitation phase if the child is tolerating feeds.
Follow-up visits should assess weight gain, morbidity, and adherence to the micronutrient plan.
Deworming and integration with community health services, such as IFA and vitamin A prophylaxis, are essential.
Non-response to rehabilitation should prompt evaluation for tuberculosis, chronic diarrhea syndromes, and social risk factors.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: A discharge micronutrient plan should specify what continues at home (multivitamins/minerals, vitamin D and calcium if indicated, iron if the child is in rehabilitation phase and tolerating feeds), and what should not be restarted prematurely.
